verses.text: Saul is introduced as tall, handsome, head and shoulders above everyone — every inch a king to the eye. The people wanted a monarch like the nations, and they get one who looks the part. But God will soon teach Israel that He does not see as man sees.

Saul is introduced as tall, handsome, head and shoulders above everyone — every inch a king to the eye. The people wanted a monarch like the nations, and they get one who looks the part. But God will soon teach Israel that He does not see as man sees.

Saul sets out merely to find his father's lost donkeys, with no idea God is steering his steps toward a throne. The LORD often launches His greatest purposes through the smallest, most ordinary errands. No day is too plain for God to be at work within it.

Saul sets out merely to find his father's lost donkeys, with no idea God is steering his steps toward a throne. The LORD often launches His greatest purposes through the smallest, most ordinary errands. No day is too plain for God to be at work within it.

It is Saul's servant who suggests seeking the man of God in the city. Wise counsel can come from the humblest quarter. Saul listens, and the search for donkeys quietly becomes a journey to meet a prophet — God guiding the whole way through a servant's simple word.

Young women drawing water point them to Samuel, just arrived for a sacrifice. Every small detail — the timing, the directions, the meeting — is being arranged. What looks to Saul like a string of coincidences is the careful hand of God bringing him exactly where He means him to be.

The day before, God had told Samuel a man would come, 'and thou shalt anoint him to be captain.' When Saul appears, the LORD says, 'this same shall reign over my people.' The meeting Saul stumbled into, God had appointed in advance. Providence always runs ahead of us.

Samuel tells Saul the donkeys are found and hints that Israel's desire rests on him. Saul protests his smallness: 'am not I a Benjamite, of the smallest of the tribes?' He begins, at least, in humility. The danger of any calling lies not in how it starts but how it ends.

Samuel seats Saul at the head of the guests and sets before him a portion kept back on purpose. God honours the man He has chosen, even before the people know it. The quiet preparations of grace go before the public day of calling.

Samuel seats Saul at the head of the guests and sets before him a portion kept back on purpose. God honours the man He has chosen, even before the people know it. The quiet preparations of grace go before the public day of calling.

As they part, Samuel says, 'stand thou still a while, that I may shew thee the word of God.' Before the great task is named, Saul must pause and hear from the LORD. No calling is safe to take up until we have first stood still to receive God's word.
